seo.title: Rayong: Complete Travel Guide 2026 — Koh Samet, Beaches & Tropical Fruit
seo.metaDescription: Plan your Rayong trip with our complete 2026 guide. Koh Samet island, Mae Ramphueng Beach, Sunthorn Phu birthplace, best hotels, and practical tips for Thailand's Eastern Seaboard.

overview: Rayong is Bangkok's go-to weekend escape — a working Gulf coast province that most foreign travelers pass through on the way to Koh Samet island without realizing the mainland has plenty of its own appeal. The province produces more tropical fruit than almost anywhere in Thailand: May to July, roadside stalls overflow with durian, rambutan, mangosteen, and longan at orchard-direct prices. Mae Ramphueng Beach stretches 12 uninterrupted kilometers — one of the longest beaches on the Gulf — and barely sees foreign tourists. The town of Ban Phe is the gateway to Koh Samet, with five piers offering hourly ferries. Rayong City is the birthplace of Sunthorn Phu, Thailand's most revered poet, honored in a park filled with statues of characters from his works. The province also produces Thailand's finest nam pla (fish sauce) and shrimp paste, and its seafood markets rival anything on the Eastern Seaboard. For independent travelers willing to go slightly off the Bangkok-Pattaya-Koh Samet conveyor belt, Rayong mainland rewards with local prices, genuine Thai character, and far fewer foreign tourists than its neighbors.
highlights: Koh Samet — a beautiful national park island with white-sand beaches, just 45 minutes by ferry from Ban Phe pier, accessible year-round
Mae Ramphueng Beach — 12 km of uncrowded Gulf coast beach, soft khaki sand and calm water, the longest contiguous beach in eastern Thailand
Tropical fruit orchards — May to July, durian, rambutan, mangosteen and longan at roadside stalls; orchard tours available near Wang Chan and Klaeng
Khao Laem Ya National Park — mainland headland with forest trails, sea views, and the park that also covers Koh Samet (200 THB national park fee)
Sunthorn Phu Park — statues of characters from Thailand's greatest poet's works, born in Ban Kram village on the Rayong coast
Ban Phe Market — fresh seafood, dried squid (a Rayong specialty), shrimp paste, and nam pla fish sauce direct from local producers
Rayong Aquarium — educational marine exhibits with a glass-tunnel walk-through and local Gulf of Thailand species
Koh Munnork — a small island off Mae Phim with crystal-clear water and coral; accessible by speedboat, far quieter than Koh Samet
Laem Mae Phim Beach — a curving bay beach south of Ban Phe with water sports, kayak rental, and the Rayong Marriott Resort on its doorstep
Wat Pa Pradu — a revered forest temple in Rayong province known for its ancient trees and peaceful meditation grounds

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov-Feb: Dry season on the Gulf coast with clear skies, calm seas, and ideal beach conditions. Koh Samet is accessible year-round but at its best November to April. December-January is busiest with highest hotel prices. February is ideal: good weather, slightly fewer crowds.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar-Apr: Still dry and warm (32-35C). Beach conditions remain good. Hotel prices ease slightly compared to peak season. Songkran (Thai New Year) in April brings festive atmosphere. Tropical fruit season has not yet started.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: May-Oct: Southwest monsoon brings occasional showers but Rayong is drier than the Andaman coast — the Gulf shields the eastern seaboard from the worst monsoon weather. May-July is tropical fruit season with durian, rambutan, and mangosteen at peak. Koh Samet ferries still run daily even in monsoon. Hotel prices drop 20-40%.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for guaranteed beach weather and the best Koh Samet conditions. May to July is excellent for fruit lovers — combine beach time with orchard visits when durian is at its peak. Rayong is notably more pleasant in low season than the Andaman coast because the Gulf keeps conditions milder.

topAttractions.name: Koh Samet
topAttractions.description: Rayong's crown jewel — a national park island 45 minutes by ferry from Ban Phe pier with brilliant white-sand beaches, calm turquoise water, and a laid-back atmosphere. The east coast has the best beaches: Hat Sai Kaew (the main White Sand Beach) is busy but beautiful; Ao Phai and Ao Tubtim are quieter with good snorkeling. All beaches are within the Khao Laem Ya-Mu Ko Samet National Park.
topAttractions.location: 45 minutes by ferry from Ban Phe pier, Rayong
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB national park fee (~$6 USD), valid for one week
tips: Take the slow ferry (50-70 THB) from Nuanthip Pier for the best price, or speedboat (150-300 THB) for speed
Hat Sai Kaew is the main beach but Ao Cho and Ao Nuan are quieter and prettier
Stay overnight to experience the island when day-trippers leave — evenings are magical

topAttractions.name: Mae Ramphueng Beach
topAttractions.description: One of Thailand's longest beaches — a 12 km strip of soft sand along the Gulf of Thailand between Ban Phe and Laem Mae Phim. Almost completely unknown to foreign tourists, this is where Bangkok families come on weekends. Calm, clean water makes it safe for swimming. Beachside restaurants serve fresh grilled seafood at local prices. The scale and emptiness relative to other Thai beaches is striking.
topAttractions.location: Route 3183, between Ban Phe and Laem Mae Phim, Rayong
tips: The middle section (around Hat Mae Ramphueng OTOP market) has the best seafood restaurants
Walk north from Ban Phe about 2 km for the quietest stretches
Weekdays are nearly deserted — weekends bring Thai families but it never feels overcrowded given the scale

topAttractions.name: Khao Laem Ya National Park
topAttractions.description: The mainland section of the national park that also covers Koh Samet. The Khao Laem Ya headland offers forest hiking trails through lowland jungle with sea views and a lighthouse at the tip. The park visitor center near Ban Phe provides information on the islands. Good birdwatching and a quieter alternative to Koh Samet for a half-day of nature.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB for foreigners (same ticket covers Koh Samet)
tips: Combine with a Koh Samet day trip — the ticket is valid for a week
The headland trail is 2-3 km and takes about 1.5 hours

topAttractions.name: Sunthorn Phu Park
topAttractions.description: A public park honoring Thailand's most celebrated poet, Sunthorn Phu (1786-1855), who was born in Ban Kram village on the Rayong coast. Whimsical statues of characters from his epic poem Phra Aphai Mani fill the park — a mermaid, a prince, a giant, and a hermit rising from the sea. The park is a pilgrimage site for Thai literature lovers and a pleasant green space with coastal views.
topAttractions.location: Route 3149, Ko Kram, Mueang Rayong (near Ban Kram village)
tips: The large mermaid statue is the most photogenic — best in morning light
The park is most atmospheric on weekdays when it's quiet
Combine with a trip to Mae Ramphueng Beach — they're 10 km apart

topAttractions.name: Ban Phe Market & Seafood Pier
topAttractions.description: The lively market town that is the gateway to Koh Samet. Ban Phe's pier area is packed with seafood restaurants, dried fish and squid stalls, and vendors selling shrimp paste, nam pla fish sauce, and tropical fruit. Rayong dried squid is famous across Thailand — Bangkok visitors stock up at Ban Phe before heading home. The morning market (5-9 AM) is the most authentic.
tips: Dried squid (pla muek haeng) is Rayong's most famous product — buy from vendors near the pier for best prices
The morning market starts around 5 AM with fresh seafood direct from overnight fishing boats
Eat at the open-air restaurants by the pier before or after your Koh Samet ferry

topAttractions.name: Rayong Tropical Fruit Orchards
topAttractions.description: Rayong province is one of Thailand's top tropical fruit regions. May to July, the roadsides around Wang Chan, Klaeng, and Baan Khai are lined with stalls selling durian, rambutan, mangosteen, longan, and lychee at orchard-direct prices — far cheaper than Bangkok. Some orchards offer tours and tastings. The durian variety 'Mon Thong' from Rayong is prized across the country.
topAttractions.location: Route 36 and surrounding areas, Wang Chan and Klaeng districts
tips: Durian season: May-June. Rambutan and mangosteen: June-July. Check the specific fruit season before visiting
Mon Thong (golden pillow) is the premium durian — creamy, mild, and the most expensive
Ask for a durian tasting at roadside stalls — sellers will open one to let you taste before buying

travelTips: Koh Samet is accessible year-round (unlike the Andaman islands which close in monsoon season) — it's Rayong's main draw and the reason most foreign visitors come
Ferries to Koh Samet run hourly from Ban Phe's Nuanthip Pier and Sribanphe Pier (5-7 AM to 7 PM daily) — 50-70 THB slow ferry or 150 THB fast catamaran
National park fee for Koh Samet is 200 THB for foreigners — valid for one week and covers both the island and Khao Laem Ya mainland park
Rayong City (the provincial capital) is 30 km from Ban Phe — most tourists skip it but the Sunthorn Phu Museum and fresh markets are worth a half day
Driving from Bangkok takes 3-3.5 hours via Highway 36 — rent a car for maximum flexibility with the fruit orchards and beaches along the way
Rayong is Thailand's largest industrial zone (Map Ta Phut) — stick to the coastal areas west of the industrial zone for a pleasant experience
Bus from Bangkok's Ekkamai Bus Terminal to Ban Phe takes 3-4 hours, costs 160-200 THB — services run throughout the day
Tropical fruit season (May-July) is one of the best reasons to visit — prices at roadside stalls are 30-50% of Bangkok supermarket prices
gettingThere: From Bangkok, Rayong is 3-3.5 hours by car or bus via Highway 36. Buses from Ekkamai Bus Terminal (Eastern Bus Terminal) to Rayong City cost 160-200 THB and run frequently; buses to Ban Phe (for Koh Samet) take 3.5-4 hours. The nearest airport is U-Tapao International Airport (UTP) near Pattaya, 80 km west of Rayong — with domestic flights from Bangkok and connections to some international hubs. Alternatively, fly into Bangkok's Suvarnabhumi Airport (BKK) and take a direct bus to Rayong, 2 hours from the airport.
gettingAround: Rayong province is best explored by rental car or motorbike — public transport between the coast, fruit orchards, and the provincial capital is infrequent. In Ban Phe, everything is walkable near the pier. Songthaew (shared trucks) run along the coast road between Ban Phe, Mae Ramphueng Beach, and Laem Mae Phim for 20-40 THB. Taxis and songthaew are available from Rayong Bus Terminal. Grab ride-hailing works in Rayong City and Ban Phe. On Koh Samet, songthaew trucks connect the beaches (30-100 THB per ride) or rent a motorcycle (300-400 THB/day).
